metadata.title: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = localized packet / initial; CRIMSON = spread packet / later / fast components
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Gaussian-integral derivation, no chirp/complex-width algebra, no phase-vs-group recap. Wonder register: dispersion spreading from first principles.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: A baseball thrown in a straight line keeps its shape. A quantum particle released as a tidy packet inevitably smears wider — and the tighter you pin it at the start, the faster it blurs.

card.copy: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own

beats.narration_text: A localized quantum particle is not a single pure frequency. It is a bundle of many momenta packed together, each component a different wave. The tighter you localize it, the wider that bundle of momenta has to be.

production_viz.label: narrow packet in x ↔ wide spread in k (momentum)

beats.narration_text: The packet starts tight. No force acts on it. No collision happens. After some time it has smeared across a much wider region. How does a free particle blur on its own?

card.copy: A tidy quantum packet. No forces. No collisions. It still spreads wider with time. How does a free particle blur on its own?

beats.narration_text: For a free particle, energy scales as k squared — momentum squared over twice the mass. Higher momentum means higher speed. The fast components in the packet race ahead; the slow ones fall behind.

production_viz.label: E ∝ k² — high-k waves outrun low-k waves

beats.narration_text: As time passes, the fast waves pull ahead and the slow waves lag. The packet stretches. Each Fourier component travels at its own phase velocity and they fan apart — the packet has no choice but to widen.

production_viz.label: packet stretching — fast components ahead, slow behind

beats.narration_text: The tighter the original packet, the wider the momentum bundle, and the larger the speed difference between fastest and slowest components. Tight confinement at the start guarantees rapid spreading afterward.

production_viz.label: narrow σ₀ → wide Δk → faster fan-out

beats.narration_text: The time for the width to double scales as the initial size squared times the particle mass, divided by h-bar. A heavier particle spreads more slowly. A tighter packet spreads more quickly. Both follow from the same quadratic dispersion.

production_viz.label: t_double ∝ mσ₀²/ℏ — heavier or wider → slower spreading

beats.narration_text: This is pure quantum dispersion — not heat, not disorder. A photon doesn't spread this way because light has a linear dispersion: all frequencies travel at the same speed. An electron's quadratic dispersion is what makes it blur.

production_viz.label: linear (photon, no spread) vs quadratic (electron, spreads)

beats.narration_text: An electron pinned to 1 nanometer at time zero doubles its width in about 0.3 femtoseconds — the time it takes light to cross an atom. Pin it to 0.1 nanometers and the doubling time shrinks to 3 attoseconds.
A localized particle is a bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle fans apart. The tighter you started, the faster it spreads.

card.copy: Localized = bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle fans apart. Tighter start: faster spread.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
